nutrition_facts.title: Toy Breed Nutrition
facts: Toy breeds have fast metabolisms and need calorie-dense food relative to body size
Small stomachs mean 3-4 small meals per day prevent hypoglycemia
Toy breeds are prone to dental disease — kibble size and dental chews matter
High-quality protein is critical to maintain lean muscle mass in tiny frames

nutrition_facts.title: Small Breed Nutrition
facts: Small breeds need higher calorie density per pound than large breeds
Smaller kibble pieces improve chewing and digestion
Small breeds often live longer — antioxidant-rich foods support longevity
Omega-3 fatty acids support coat health, which is often a concern in small breeds

nutrition_facts.title: Medium Breed Nutrition
facts: Medium breeds are the most adaptable — a balanced all-life-stages formula often works well
Active medium breeds (Border Collies, Australian Shepherds) need higher protein ratios
Maintaining a healthy weight is key — medium breeds can gain weight easily if overfed
Omega-3 and omega-6 balance supports coat and brain function

nutrition_facts.title: Large & Giant Breed Nutrition
facts: Large breeds need controlled calcium-to-phosphorus ratios to prevent rapid bone growth that stresses joints
Glucosamine and chondroitin support joint health which is critical for large and giant dogs
Large breeds are prone to bloat (GDV) — feed 2-3 smaller meals instead of one large meal
DHA and EPA omega-3 fatty acids reduce joint inflammation in large breeds
Giant breeds reach skeletal maturity later — avoid over-supplementing calcium in puppies

nutrition_facts.title: Puppy Nutrition (Under 1 Year)
facts: Puppies need twice the calories per pound compared to adult dogs to fuel rapid growth
DHA is essential for brain and eye development in puppies
Puppies need more protein (22-32% DM) than adults for muscle and tissue development
Frequent meals (3-4x daily) prevent hypoglycemia and support sustained energy
Avoid adult formulas — calcium levels need to be carefully calibrated for growth plates

nutrition_facts.title: Junior Dog Nutrition (1-2 Years)
facts: Junior dogs are transitioning from puppy to adult — protein needs remain high
Energy levels peak at this stage — active play burns many calories
This is the ideal time to establish healthy weight baselines
B vitamins support the nervous system during continued neurological maturation

nutrition_facts.title: Adult Dog Nutrition (2-7 Years)
facts: Adult dogs need balanced macros: 18-25% protein, 10-15% fat, rest from carbohydrates and fiber
Antioxidants (vitamin E, vitamin C) help protect cells from oxidative stress
Prebiotics and probiotics maintain gut microbiome diversity and immune function
Maintaining lean body mass requires adequate protein even in less-active adults

nutrition_facts.title: Senior Dog Nutrition (7+ Years)
facts: Senior dogs have reduced metabolism — calorie intake should decrease by 20-30%
Higher protein (not lower) preserves muscle mass that naturally declines with age
Antioxidants (CoQ10, vitamin E) combat oxidative stress linked to aging
Joint supplements (glucosamine, chondroitin) become essential as cartilage wears
Phosphorus restriction may be needed if kidney function declines
Digestibility matters more — easily digestible proteins like eggs and white fish are ideal

nutrition_facts.title: Low Activity Dogs
facts: Low-activity dogs need roughly 1.2x their resting energy requirement (RER)
Excess calories quickly lead to obesity — high fiber foods promote satiety without excess calories
Lean proteins prevent muscle loss while keeping fat content low
L-carnitine helps convert stored fat to energy, supporting healthy weight

nutrition_facts.title: Moderately Active Dogs
facts: Moderately active dogs need approximately 1.6x their RER
A balance of protein and complex carbohydrates sustains steady energy throughout the day
Post-walk meals (vs pre-walk) reduce bloat risk, especially in large breeds
B vitamins support energy metabolism during regular exercise

nutrition_facts.title: Active Dogs
facts: Active dogs need 2x their RER — energy demands double with vigorous daily exercise
High protein (28-30% DM) repairs micro muscle tears from exercise
Fat is a more calorie-dense energy source than carbs — healthy fats are essential
Electrolyte balance (sodium, potassium, magnesium) supports muscle function
Recovery meals rich in protein and antioxidants reduce post-exercise inflammation

nutrition_facts.title: Athletic & Working Dogs
facts: Working and sporting dogs can need 2.5-5x RER depending on workload
Fat should provide 50-65% of calories for endurance work — fat oxidation is efficient
During intense periods, increase meal frequency to 3x daily
Adequate hydration is critical — working dogs lose significant water through panting
Recovery nutrition: protein within 30-60 minutes of activity supports muscle repair

nutrition_facts.title: Weight Management
facts: Overweight dogs have 2x the risk of diabetes, joint disease, and heart problems
Target 1-2% body weight loss per week — crash dieting causes muscle loss
High-fiber foods (pumpkin, green beans, carrots) increase satiety with fewer calories
Measure meals by weight, not volume — cup size varies significantly between kibbles
L-carnitine supplementation helps mobilize stored fat for energy

nutrition_facts.title: Joint Health Support
facts: Glucosamine (500-1000mg/day for medium dogs) stimulates cartilage repair
Chondroitin sulfate prevents cartilage breakdown and retains water in joints
Green-lipped mussel is one of the richest natural sources of ETA omega-3 for joint inflammation
Boswellia serrata extract reduces joint inflammation without NSAID side effects
Excess weight dramatically accelerates joint wear — every extra pound = 4 lbs of joint stress
Vitamin C supports collagen synthesis — critical for cartilage structure

nutrition_facts.title: Sensitive Stomach & Food Allergies
facts: The most common canine food allergens are beef, dairy, wheat, chicken, and soy
Novel proteins (venison, rabbit, duck, kangaroo) reduce allergic reactions when switching
Hydrolyzed protein diets break proteins into fragments too small to trigger immune response
Probiotics (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium) restore gut flora disrupted by food intolerances
Pumpkin and sweet potato are hypoallergenic carb sources that soothe inflamed guts
Elimination diets need 8-12 weeks before results are assessable

nutrition_facts.title: Coat & Skin Health
facts: Omega-3 (EPA/DHA) and omega-6 (linoleic acid) balance is the #1 factor in coat quality
Zinc deficiency causes dull coat and flaky skin — often seen in northern breeds
Biotin supports keratin production for strong, shiny fur
Vitamin E acts as an antioxidant protecting skin cell membranes from damage
Salmon oil (0.5-1 tsp per 10 lbs) significantly improves coat shine within 4-6 weeks

nutrition_facts.title: Dental Health
facts: 80% of dogs over 3 years have some degree of periodontal disease
Crunchy kibble creates mild mechanical abrasion that slows tartar buildup
Specially designed dental chews (VOHC-approved) reduce plaque by 20-70%
Raw meaty bones (appropriate size, supervised) naturally clean teeth
Parsley and fresh mint naturally freshen breath by neutralizing sulfur compounds
Avoid sugar-containing treats — bacteria ferment sugars to acids that erode enamel

nutrition_facts.title: Heart Health Support
facts: Taurine deficiency has been linked to dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) — ensure adequate intake
L-carnitine transports fatty acids into cardiac muscle cells for energy
Sodium restriction may be needed in dogs with diagnosed heart disease
Omega-3 fatty acids reduce cardiac inflammation and improve heart muscle function
Coenzyme Q10 supports mitochondrial energy production in heart cells
Grain-free diets with legumes as main protein have been investigated for DCM risk

nutrition_facts.title: Protein Quality Matters
facts: Named animal proteins (chicken, salmon, beef) are more digestible than meat meals or by-products
Eggs have a biological value (BV) of 100 — the gold standard for protein quality
Amino acid profile matters: taurine, L-carnitine, methionine, and lysine are key canine essentials
The first ingredient listed should be a whole protein source, not a grain or legume

nutrition_facts.title: Hydration & Digestive Health
facts: Dogs need approximately 1 oz of water per pound of body weight daily
Wet food (70-80% moisture) significantly boosts total water intake
Pumpkin is a digestive powerhouse — soluble fiber relieves both constipation and diarrhea
Bone broth (low sodium) encourages picky eaters and adds collagen and minerals
Fermented foods (plain kefir, plain goat yogurt) introduce beneficial bacteria
